Difficult to get to due to steep slope from road; however, are known to be very tasty apples. Surrounded by blackberries; fight the vines, eat the berries, pick the pomes, and be on your way! Falling Fruit season: September-September.
Apple tree next to the highway, but might be difficult to get to. Watch out for the blackberries and the steep slope from the road! Ripe mid-September? Falling Fruit season: September-September.

Along Fishtrap Road you can find a number of apple and plum trees; however, some are in people's yards, and some aren't. Dont be rude; if you're not sure, ask first. Our experience is that people with fruit trees are of the most generous and kind-hearted spiri
A good-looking apple tree, probably used by residents, however. Find out for sure, help them with the harvest and make new friends!

Downhill from St. James Episcopal, you find several plum trees growing like brother and sister. Delicious! A bit further down the hill and across the street is an apple tree, as well; and, of course, also present are blackberries. Pick them all when they're ri

Sources say there's an apple near here, the intersection of 6th and Gould. We don't know if it's public or private, small or large, abundant or paltry, delicious or gravenstein; we need your input, Coquillians! Enlighten our obscurious ways!
Medium-size apples on untended tree. Unsure of ripening time; late September? Falling Fruit season: September-October.

Along this strip of the road you can find several cherries, plums, apples, hazelnuts, and a couple rather lovely huckleberry bushes! Scattered along both sides of the road; drive slowly (bike instead) and keep your eyes open.
Two black cherry trees producing abundantly. They're small but packed with all-out cherry goodness, and they dry pretty well too, so pluck plenty.

Downhill from St. James Episcopal, you find several plum trees growing like brother and sister. Delicious! A bit further down the hill and across the street is an apple tree, as well; and, of course, also present are blackberries. Pick them all when they're ri

Are they filberts or are they hazelnuts? Only Ish knows, but not this writer. Hazard a guess, probably filberts. Either way, they've gots nuts for the picking. I don't know what botanists would call them, so I'll call them delicious!
A small stand of five or six filberts growing tightly together. Appears to be public property, but might not be. Falling Fruit season: September-September.

Along this strip of the road you can find several cherries, plums, apples, hazelnuts, and a couple rather lovely huckleberry bushes! Scattered along both sides of the road; drive slowly (bike instead) and keep your eyes open.

Along this strip of the road you can find several cherries, plums, apples, hazelnuts, and a couple rather lovely huckleberry bushes! Scattered along both sides of the road; drive slowly (bike instead) and keep your eyes open.
Two White Oak trees growing symbolically in front of the courthouse. Put them to practical use, and gather them acorns!
Possible persimmon tree, approximate location. Might be on sixth, north side of road for sure.

Along Fishtrap Road you can find a number of apple and plum trees; however, some are in people's yards, and some aren't. Dont be rude; if you're not sure, ask first. Our experience is that people with fruit trees are of the most generous and kind-hearted spiri
7 or 8 plum abundantly producing trees with small fruit of varying color and type. Easily picked from the drooping limbs. Falling Fruit season: July-July.
Dark red plum growing as a street tree. Abundant crop of medium-sized fruit. Falling Fruit season: July-July.

The street tree strip shines again, this time with three English walnuts and an abundantly producing plum tree chock full of small, red plums most likely ripe in late July. Falling Fruit season: July-unknown.

Three plum trees growing as street trees. Small to medium-size fruit, abundant producers. Falling Fruit season: July-July.
There are a couple plum trees growing on the east side of the road here. Not too abundant, and unsure of ripening; definitely worth putting in some further exploration.
Medium-size plums found fairly abundantly on this purple-leafed tree. Falling Fruit season: July-July.
At least 5 abundantly producing plum trees adorn the west side of N Ivy Street. We assume they ripen late July, but why don't you find out for sure? Falling Fruit season: July-August.
If those plums down the hill on Ivy weren't enough to satiate, here's one more! Also ripens late July (we think). Falling Fruit season: July-August.
